name: Liège (Belgium)

colors.symbolism: the field of the historic arms of the Prince-Bishopric of Liège

description: A banner of the provincial arms quartered into four fields representing the historical territories that make up the modern province: a yellow perron on a red field for the Prince-Bishopric of Liège, three white fleurs-de-lis on a red field for Bouillon, a green and gold quarter for Franchimont, and additional charges from the historic territories of Loon and Horne; the four quarters arranged around the central provincial shield.
history: The Province of Liège has no officially adopted flag; a banner of the provincial arms is used unofficially as the flag of the province. The current provincial arms were officially granted on 1 February 1947 and quarter the arms of the Prince-Bishopric of Liège (the perron on red), Bouillon, Franchimont, and Loon — the historic territories whose combination forms the modern province. A formal flag proposal by Léon Nyssen was submitted to the provincial authorities on 30 October 2001 but not adopted.

features.symbolism: the four quarters of the historic territories making up the province; the dominant red field is that of the Prince-Bishopric of Liège

features.role: banner of the quartered provincial arms — Liège (perron or on gules), Bouillon, Franchimont, and Loon — granted 1 February 1947
features.symbolism: combines the heraldry of the historic Prince-Bishopric of Liège, Duchy of Bouillon, Marquisate of Franchimont and County of Loon, whose former territories make up the modern province
